items.q: What happens to electrons when sodium reacts with chlorine?
items.correct: Sodium loses one electron to chlorine; the resulting Na+ and Cl- ions attract electrostatically.
items.misconception: Sodium and chlorine share an electron between them.
items.random_wrong: Protons move from chlorine to sodium to balance the charges.
items.why: Students over-apply the covalent (sharing) model to all bonding because sharing is introduced first and feels intuitive.

items.correct: Delocalised electrons are free to move through the lattice of positive ions, carrying charge.
items.misconception: Metal atoms themselves move to carry the current.
items.random_wrong: Metals contain naturally moving protons that act as charge carriers.
items.why: Students picture conduction as 'something moving through the wire' and default to whole atoms, since they don't fully grasp delocalisation.

items.q: Does a catalyst affect the position of equilibrium?
items.correct: No — it speeds up both the forward and reverse reactions equally, so equilibrium is reached faster but at the same position.
items.misconception: A catalyst shifts the equilibrium towards the products.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst increases the yield by being consumed in the forward reaction only.
items.why: Students associate 'catalyst' with 'helps the reaction' and assume that means 'pushes towards products' rather than 'speeds up both directions equally'.

items.q: Why acidify with HNO3 before AgNO3?
items.why: It's to remove carbonate interference. “to increase ions” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's more sensitive and accurate. “slower” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Moles AgCl from 0.02 mol AgNO3 reacting fully with NaCl?
items.why: The real figure is 0.02. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
